description: Cold archive, rarely queried (~5/month), append-only, large well-sized files. Correct answer: Z (do nothing or minimal), F (periodic snapshot expiry). No compaction, no sort, no z-order.

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: batch-tolerant — minutes or more is fine, nobody is waiting
interview_answers.query_frequency: almost never — maybe 3–5 times per year for audit purposes
interview_answers.freshness_sla: once-a-day or slower is fine, no real-time requirement
interview_answers.cost_priority: storage cost — minimize the monthly storage bill
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: append-only forever, no updates or deletes
interview_answers.time_travel_need: only the latest state matters, snapshot history is just storage cost
interview_answers.lifecycle: cold archive — rarely touched, optimize for cheap storage

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to do nothing or at most expire old snapshots (snapshot expiry / expire_snapshots). Because the table is a cold archive with only 3–5 queries per year and files are already well-sized at 300 MB, data file compaction (rewrite_data_files / optimize) would cost more compute than it ever saves. Sort compaction, z-order, bloom filters, and partition evolution are all wasteful at this query volume. Only snapshot expiry is justified if there are many stale snapshots. A recommendation to run compaction, sort, or z-order is incorrect.

description: Streaming table, 30-second commit cadence, 1 MB files spread across 20 partitions per commit (thin spread). Queried constantly by tenant_id + event_time for interactive dashboards. Correct: A (bin-pack cold partitions), C (z-order tenant_id + event_time), J (write-time tuning distribution-mode=hash), F (aggressive snapshot expiry).

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: interactive — sub-second to seconds, user-facing dashboards
interview_answers.query_frequency: constantly — hundreds of queries per hour from many consumers
interview_answers.freshness_sla: minutes is acceptable — 5-minute delay is fine
interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — we optimize for speed, not cost
interview_answers.time_travel_need: only the latest state matters, history is exhaust
interview_answers.lifecycle: hot — actively used, optimize for speed

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to compact the 1 MB small files (bin-pack or sort compaction via rewrite_data_files), apply z-order or sort on tenant_id + event_time to enable data skipping, tune write-time distribution (distribution-mode=hash or similar) to prevent future thin-spread small files, and expire snapshots aggressively. The table has 5,000 small 1 MB files spread thin — compaction is urgent. A recommendation that skips compaction or says 'do nothing' is incorrect.

description: Table with accumulating equality deletes from GDPR right-to-be-forgotten purges (~1M records/day). eq_delete_pressure = 10%. Queried regularly by compliance team. Correct: E1 (equality delete compaction with remove-dangling-deletes, non-optional), F (aggressive snapshot expiry to physically remove data). Must mention physical deletion and snapshot history risk.

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: responsive — seconds to minutes is acceptable
interview_answers.query_frequency: regularly — daily reports by the compliance team
interview_answers.freshness_sla: hours is fine — same-day is acceptable
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: deletes required — GDPR right-to-be-forgotten, individual row deletions on regulatory deadlines
interview_answers.time_travel_need: only the latest state matters — retained snapshots are a GDPR liability, not a feature
interview_answers.retention_policy: GDPR right-to-be-forgotten — must physically remove rows within 30 days of request

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must include: (1) rewrite_data_files with remove-dangling-deletes to physically remove deleted rows from data files — this is non-optional for GDPR compliance; (2) expire_snapshots aggressively (preferably retain_last=1 or near-current only) because retained snapshot history means deleted rows can still be read from older snapshots, creating a GDPR liability. The recommendation must explicitly warn that logical delete files are not enough for compliance — physical removal requires both compaction and snapshot expiry. A recommendation that omits compaction or omits snapshot expiry, or that does not mention the GDPR/compliance risk of snapshot history, is incorrect.

description: Well-maintained table partitioned by event_date (daily). But 98% of queries filter on tenant_id only — partition_prune_rate = 0.0. Every query scans the full 12.5 GB table. Profile looks healthy (256 MB files, no deletes, no small files). The problem is invisible without the workload. Correct: D (partition evolution to bucket/hash by tenant_id) or B/C (sort/z-order by tenant_id as a cheaper alternative). Must identify tenant_id as the key column to optimize on.

interview_answers.query_frequency: constantly — 1000+ queries per month from analysts
interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — the query bills are getting large

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to fix the partition-column mismatch. Queries filter almost exclusively on tenant_id, but the table is partitioned by event_date — so partition pruning never fires (prune_rate=0.0) and every query full-table scans 12.5 GB. The recommendation must identify tenant_id as the key column to optimize on, and propose either: (a) partition evolution to bucket or hash-partition by tenant_id (strongest fix, eliminates full scans), or (b) sort compaction / z-order by tenant_id (cheaper alternative, enables data skipping without re-partitioning). A recommendation that ignores tenant_id, focuses only on the file sizes (which are already healthy), or recommends snapshot expiry as the main action is incorrect.

description: Small table with 5 well-sized 256 MB files but 1200 hourly snapshots (1+ year of history). No small files, no deletes. Queried weekly for reports with good partition pruning. Correct: F (snapshot expiry — the only real problem), G (manifest rewrite). Must NOT recommend data file compaction (files are already well-sized).

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: batch-tolerant — weekly reports, minutes is fine
interview_answers.time_travel_need: nice to have — keep 2 weeks of history for rollback

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is snapshot expiry (expire_snapshots / expire_snapshots with retention_threshold) as the primary action — the table has 1,200 hourly snapshots accumulated over more than a year, causing metadata bloat. Optionally also rewrite manifests (optimize_manifests). The data files themselves are perfectly sized at 256 MB — data file compaction (rewrite_data_files / EXECUTE optimize) is not needed and would waste compute. A recommendation that prescribes data file compaction, sort, or z-order as a main action is incorrect; the files are already well-sized.
